q: A camera is an optical instrument that forms and records an ___ of an object.
The image in a camera may be recorded on film (or) it may be detected by an ___ that stores the image digitally.
Regardless of how the image is recorded, all cameras form images in the same ___.
A convex lens focuses the light from the object to form a real ___ image on the film or electronic sensor.
A ___ is placed behind the convex lens to control the amount of light energy falling on the film.
The ___ is a circular opening through which the light passes.
The size or the diameter of the aperture is expressed in terms of ___.
The ___ of a camera is the ratio of the focal length of the lens to the diameter of aperture.
The smaller the f-number, the ___ the aperture is.
When taking a photograph in bright light a large f-number (___ aperture) must be used.
A ___ controls the time during which light enters the camera through the lens.
The shutter speed is expressed in terms of the ___.
The shorter the exposure time, the ___ the shutter speed is.
A camera consists of a ___ by which the distance between the lens and the film can be adjusted to obtain a sharp image on the film.
The film at the back of the camera is actually a ___ which receives the image.
The film consists of a thin layer of ___ of silver.
In a digital camera, the electronic sensor is made up of millions of tiny ___ which respond to light.
Human eye consists of ___ which are capable of receiving visual images and sending the information to the brain.
Since the eyelid opens and closes the eye, it is similar to the ___ of the camera.
The front portion of the eye is covered by a tough, transparent membrane called the ___.
The region between the cornea and the eyelens is filled with a transparent liquid called the ___.
The region between the eyelens and the retina contains a transparent liquid called the ___.
The iris which is the ___ of the eye controls the amount of light energy entering the eye.
The ___ is in the centre of the iris and the light passes through it.
The ___ adjusts the size of the pupil in the eye.
The pupil automatically ___ in dim light and contracts in bright light.
The ___ are attached to the eyelens.
The ciliary muscles can vary the ___ of the eyelens, thus alter the focal length of the lens.
When near objects are viewed, the ciliary muscles ___ and permit the eyelens to form a more convex shape.
When distant objects are viewed, the ciliary muscles ___ and the eyelens becomes less convex.
The ___ is the light sensitive area of the cells, at the back of the eye.
The retina consists of ___ and ___.
The ___ which is the most light-sensitive spot on the retina is used for viewing very small objects.
Since the yellow spot consists of ___ exclusively, the small objects can be seen clearly.
There is a spot on the retina which has no rods or cones; this spot is called the ___.
The light energy falling on the retina is transmitted to the brain by means of the ___.
The cornea, the aqueous humour, the eyelens and the vitreous humour are ___.
The image of an object formed on the retina is real, inverted and ___ than the size of the object.
The common defects of vision are ___ (hyperopia) and nearsightedness (myopia).
___ is a defect of vision in which distant objects are seen clearly, but objects nearby are blurry.
For a person with farsightedness, the rays from near objects intersect at a point ___ the retina.
Farsightedness can be corrected by the use of spectacles with ___ lens or convex lens.
___ is a defect of vision in which near objects are seen clearly, but objects far away are blurry.
For a person with nearsightedness, the rays from distant objects intersect at a point ___ of the retina.
Nearsightedness can be corrected by the use of spectacles with ___ lens or concave lens.
The distance known as the least distance of distinct vision is about ___.
For normal vision the maximum distance of distinct vision is ___.
When objects at infinity are viewed, the eyes are ___.
A ___ is an optical instrument which uses lenses to make enlarged images of objects too small for the unaided eye to see.
___ is applied to analyse the formation of an image in the microscope.
A common type of microscope is a ___.
A compound microscope has at least two ___: an objective lens and an eyepiece lens.
The lens nearer the object is called the ___.
The lens through which the final image is viewed is called the ___.
The objective produces an image which is real, ___ and larger than the size of the object.
The final image formed by a compound microscope is ___ and on the same side as the object.
The ___ of a compound microscope is the product of the magnification of the objective and the magnifying power of the eyepiece.
For a large magnifying power, both the objective and the eyepiece should have short ___.
___ is a microscope with high magnification and resolution, employing electron beams in place of light.
Electron microscopes deliver a more ___ image compared to optical microscopes.
Optical microscopes use simple lenses, whereas electron microscopes use ___ or electromagnetic lenses.
Electron microscopes use electrons, which have ___ wavelengths that allows greater magnification.
The two main types of electron microscope are the ___ and the Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM).
An optical instrument used for viewing ___ objects is called a telescope.
Two types of telescope are ___ and terrestrial telescope.
The refracting astronomical telescope consists of an objective of ___ and an eyepiece of short focal length.
Galileo invented a ___.
The final image formed by the eyepiece in an astronomical telescope is virtual and ___ with respect to the distant object.
To obtain the sharp images of distant objects the objective should have a large ___.
The ___ was first suggested by Newton.
It is not convenient to use the astronomical telescope for viewing objects on the earth since the final image produced is ___.
In the terrestrial telescope a ___ lens, instead of a convex lens, is used as an eyepiece to produce a final image which is erect.
Total reflecting prisms are used in ___ to reduce the length of the terrestrial telescope.
Binoculars consist of a pair of ___ one for each eye.
___ is the branch of physics that studies the nature and properties of light.
LASER is an acronym for ___ by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.
A laser is a source of a narrow beam of intense, ___, coherent light.
One type of laser is the semiconductor laser, also known as a ___.
In practice, a laser contains three essential elements: ___, power supply, and optical cavity.
The process of supplying energy to the laser medium to excite it to emit light is called ___.
The ___ (or resonator) concentrates the light to stimulate the emission of laser radiation.
Two essential processes to initiate a laser action are stimulated emission and ___.
In ___ emission, the transition occurs when the atom in its excited state interacts with an incident photon.
Atoms undergoing stimulated emission radiate photons of the same ___ in the same direction.
For stimulated emission to occur, a ___ would be needed.
Population inversion means the population of the ___ level of a transition would have to be higher than that of the lower level.
The output laser beam is narrow, the emission is ___ and the light is monochromatic.
Coherence means that the light waves a laser contains are in ___ with one another.
Lasers are used in medical applications such as ___ surgery and destroying kidney stones.
Laser ___ are used to determine the distance to an object.
A continuous laser beam can range from a fraction of a milliwatt to around ___ in commercial lasers.
The fibre optics works on the principle of ___.
Fibre optics refers to the technology that transmits information as ___ along a glass or plastic fibre.
An optical fibre is composed of two concentric layers, called the ___ and cladding.
The core and cladding have different ___.
Total internal reflection occurs if the light strikes the core to cladding interface at an angle ___ than the critical angle.
Because of ___ and absorption, the light beam attenuates and dies off over a certain distance.
In medicine, ___ is a non-surgical procedure to examine the interior of a hollow organ.
Endoscopes use ___ to observe an image inside the body.
A ___ is a scientific instrument used to separate and measure spectral components of a physical phenomenon.
The optical spectrometer consists of light source, ___, dispersion elements and a telescope.
The collimated beam from a specific light source is dispersed by a dispersion element such as prism or ___.
When observing the dispersion of light, the user can determine the ___ for each spectral line.
In a ___ (UV-Vis spectrometer), a dispersion element separates light into a spectrum which falls on a sample.
___ is the science of measuring visible light in units weighted according to the sensitivity of the human eye.
The human eye responds only to light having wavelengths between about ___ and 700 nm.
The human eye is more sensitive to ___ of 555 nm compared to other colors.
Luminous flux (luminous power) $\Phi$ is a measurement of the ___ of visible light produced by a light source.
Luminous flux is measured in ___.
One lumen (1 lm) is equivalent to 1/683 ___ of light with a wavelength of 555 nm.
Luminous intensity $I_v$ is a measure of the luminous flux emitted per unit ___.
Luminous intensity is measured in ___ (cd) or lumen per steradian.
The ___ is the SI basic unit for luminous intensity.
A ___ $\Omega$ is a measure of the amount of the field of view from some particular point.
In SI system, a solid angle is expressed in ___.
___ $L_v$ is the luminous intensity per unit area of light travelling in a given direction.
The SI unit for luminance is ___.
___ $E_v$ is the total luminous flux incident on a surface per unit area.
The SI unit for illuminance is ___.
One ___ is equal to one lumen per metre squared.
The minimum average illuminance for reading areas in school study halls should be ___.
The illuminance $E_v$ being inversely proportional to the square of the distance $r$ is known as ___.
